story:
The Constable slides an unopened orange drink across the metal table. I’m handcuffed to a ring, it’s 2 a.m., and he wants my life story. I want a phone call I’m not allowed to make. I don’t trust him, but I’m not worried. I was told on day one that if I ever ended up in custody, I should sit tight. They’d come. I know too much for them to leave me here. So I stall. I stop answering, slacken in the chair, and turn a long day without food into something that looks like the start of a diabetic crash. He panics and calls for help. While I ride the performance, I decide how much truth I could ever tell a man in a cheap suit with cheaper cologne. Not much.

There are two kinds of people: those who protect animals and those who hurt them. No gray. I love animals. I also eat meat; that’s not cruelty. Cruelty is what happens when people are bored, angry, or empty and pick targets that can’t fight back. Maybe that’s why they found me—after a social media questionnaire, a crowdfunding campaign I ran for captive animals, or something I wrote that didn’t go away. Nothing posted ever does. However it happened, I was recruited by the Garden and handed a title that sounded like a joke until it didn’t: curator. My collection isn’t animals. It’s humans.

I travel. I find specimens who’ve sunk to the bottom: abusers, tormentors, the ones who laugh while life screams. I vet them, gather evidence, and decide if they come to the Garden. In this role I’m prosecutor, jury, and judge. Sometimes, if harm would keep spreading, I cut the process short. I don’t enjoy violence. I don’t flinch from it when stopping it means fewer victims tomorrow.

Once, a silverback did to a man what the man had done to a dog. It was brutal and not satisfying—justice rarely is—but it made a terrible kind of sense. Another time, a drunk at a bar bragged about shoving two dogs into a hot car, watching them fight until one was dead and the other fainted. He wanted to show me how he lured them. I let him show me, then locked him in my SUV’s custom hatch. I drove him across three states to the Garden, collecting enough felonies to fill a docket. But the people who could make those felonies stick answer to people who answer to my employers. Power shifts, yes, but for now, the protection holds.

During my orientation, they said humanity stopped choosing thoughtfully who should raise the next generation. Pleasure replaced purpose. Empathy thinned. Screens raised children while the compass spun. After seeing hundreds of cases, I don’t know if that’s the whole story, but it explains what I see. I keep files—paragraphs, photos, short clips—because sometimes I want to forgive. The files remind me of harm that doesn’t stop unless we stop it. The subjects don’t know it, but their presence in the Garden serves a purpose bigger than they ever did outside it: deterrence, accountability, and an end to what would have continued.

On runs back to the Garden, I cue my playlist. Captives hear it through the reinforced partition. We removed the third row after someone slipped her cuffs outside Vegas. I keep a diary so I don’t forget the costs. If you hear my playlist, I didn’t miss.

I barely feel the EMT come in until the finger prick. She tilts her wrist. The tattoo is there, where only I know to look. The cavalry has arrived.
